text:
CT CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with newly discovered large right renal mass on CT abdomen/pelvis (11.4 cm heterogeneous right renal mass with perinephric fat involvement and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy). Staging evaluation for suspected primary renal malignancy. Assess for thoracic metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest acquired following administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Images obtained in the portal venous phase with axial 1.25 mm reconstructions. Coronal and sagittal reformats generated and reviewed. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows reviewed.

COMPARISON: None available.

---

FINDINGS:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The central airways are patent. The trachea and mainstem bronchi are without intraluminal mass or significant narrowing.

There are multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules of varying size, distributed throughout all lobes. The dominant lesion is a well-defined, rounded soft tissue nodule in the right lower lobe measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm (series 4, image 87), located in the posterior basal segment. This lesion demonstrates mild heterogeneous enhancement. No central necrosis or cavitation is identified within this nodule.

Additional pulmonary nodules are as follows:
- Left upper lobe, anterior segment: solid nodule measuring 0.9 cm (series 4, image 43)
- Right upper lobe, posterior segment: solid nodule measuring 0.7 cm (series 4, image 31)
- Left lower lobe, superior segment: solid nodule measuring 0.6 cm (series 4, image 78)

All nodules are solid in attenuation, well-circumscribed, and without satellite lesions, spiculation, or ground-glass halos. No calcification is identified within any nodule. The morphology, multiplicity, and bilateral distribution of these nodules is highly concerning for hematogenous metastatic spread given the known primary renal mass.

No endobronchial lesion is identified. No lobar or segmental consolidation. No focal area of atelectasis.

PLEURAL SPACES:
No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural nodularity or thickening.

MEDIASTINUM AND HILA:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The aorta and great vessels are normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation or dissection. No mediastinal widening.

No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. The prevascular, paratracheal, subcarinal, and bilateral hilar nodal stations are not enlarged by size criteria (all nodes less than 1 cm in short axis). No discrete nodal masses.

THYMUS: Fatty infiltration of the thymus in keeping with involution. No thymic mass.

ESOPHAGUS: No esophageal thickening or mass.

CHEST WALL AND BONY THORAX:
No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions within the visualized ribs, thoracic vertebral bodies, sternum, or clavicles. No pathologic fracture. Mild degenerative changes in the lower thoracic spine with small anterior osteophytes. No bone destruction or cortical breakthrough.

UPPER ABDOMEN (limited, included on chest protocol):
The visualized upper abdomen demonstrates the known right renal mass, consistent with the dedicated abdominal CT findings. Partially imaged liver parenchyma appears homogeneous without discrete focal lesion in the visualized portions. Spleen, pancreatic tail, and left adrenal gland appear unremarkable on limited views.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules, the largest measuring 1.8 x 1.5 cm in the right lower lobe, with additional nodules in the left upper lobe (0.9 cm), right upper lobe (0.7 cm), and left lower lobe (0.6 cm). The multiplicity, bilateral distribution, and well-circumscribed morphology of these nodules is most consistent with hematogenous pulmonary metastases in the clinical context of a large right renal mass.

2. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

3. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes.

4. No suspicious osseous lesion identified within the thorax.

Findings discussed with referring clinician at time of preliminary read.
